aliases: Din Tai Fung Hot and Sour Soup

english: Din Tai Fung Hot and Sour Soup

region_of_origin: China (Sichuan / Shanxi tradition); served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Soups — Chinese clear and thickened soups

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03311069 covers hot and sour soup generically; the spicy variant with specific Din Tai Fung ingredients (black fungus, bamboo shoots, bean curd strips) is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers soups and broths as a category. No specific code exists for hot and sour soup or its spicy variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for hot and sour soup or Chinese thickened soup. Null is appropriate.

serving.natural_language_portion: One small bowl of spicy hot and sour soup as served at Din Tai Fung, approximately 300 g, containing mushrooms, bean curd strips, bamboo shoots, black fungus, and egg in a thickened, vinegar-spiced broth.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and sour soup, Chinese restaurant style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; supplemented with HPB Singapore nutrient data for Chinese soups
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Din Tai Fung spicy hot and sour soup exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for hot and sour soup (Chinese restaurant style, NDB 06932 / FDC ID 173590) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for Chinese soups. The small serving size (300 g) is estimated from typical restaurant bowl weights. Per-100 g values are derived from the USDA reference entry and adjusted for the spicy variant's slightly higher chilli and vinegar content, which does not materially alter macronutrient profile. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soups and mixed dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for thickened Chinese soups and tofu-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy hot and sour soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous entries for Chinese thickened soups and tofu-based mixed dishes, which consistently fall in the low GI range (GI 30–45) due to the low carbohydrate density, high water content, vinegar (which suppresses postprandial glucose response), and tofu/mushroom protein content. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 11.4) / 100 ≈ 4.0. Carbohydrate per serving = 3.8 g/100 g × 300 g = 11.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 290 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 870 mg sodium, representing about 38% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should treat this soup as an occasional dish and avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table, as the sodium load from a single small bowl is clinically significant.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) and moderate carbohydrate content (approximately 11.4 g per 300 g serving); vinegar content further attenuates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This soup is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI, high water content, and protein from tofu and egg, which together produce a modest and gradual postprandial glucose rise; portion control remains advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium content raises concern for blood pressure management in cardiovascular disease; saturated fat is low (0.3 g/100 g) and the soup contains beneficial plant-based ingredients including mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and black fungus.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and plant-rich ingredient profile are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who must restrict sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Hot and Sour Soup (小, small) is a classic Chinese thickened soup made with hand-sliced mushrooms, bean curd strips (tofu), tender bamboo shoots, black fungus (wood ear mushroom), and egg, suspended in a starch-thickened broth seasoned with vinegar, chilli, soy sauce, white pepper, and sesame oil. The soup achieves its characteristic flavour profile through the interplay of sharp acidity from vinegar, heat from chilli, and umami depth from mushrooms and stock. The texture is smooth and silky due to starch thickening, with soft, yielding pieces of tofu and mushroom throughout. This dish is a signature item at Din Tai Fung, the internationally acclaimed Taiwanese-origin restaurant chain, where it is served as a starter or accompaniment to dumplings and noodles. Nutritionally, the soup is low in calories (approximately 35 kcal per 100 g), moderate in protein (2.4 g/100 g from tofu and egg), low in fat (1.2 g/100 g), and low in carbohydrates (3.8 g/100 g). Its glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content is elevated at approximately 290 mg per 100 g, which is a clinical consideration for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The vinegar component has been shown in peer-reviewed literature to attenuate postprandial glucose response, adding to its suitability for diabetic dietary management. Black fungus and mushrooms contribute dietary fibre and micronutrients including iron and B vitamins.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Din Tai Fung spicy hot and sour soup exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA, ICMR-NIN). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central hot and sour soup (Chinese restaurant style) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Chinese soup data. The adaptation is defensible and the macronutrient profile is consistent across multiple reference points, but the absence of an exact match limits confidence to medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 06932, hot and sour soup, Chinese restaurant style) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for Chinese soups. No exact Din Tai Fung product-specific nutritional data is available from an approved source. 2. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate for a small restaurant bowl; actual weight may vary. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for thickened Chinese soups and tofu-based dishes; no direct measurement exists for this specific product. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA reference entry for this soup category. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA reference entry for this soup category. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA reference entry for this soup category. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in plant-based and minimally processed Chinese soups per USDA data. 8. Halal status is listed as false because the stock base (chicken or pork) used at Din Tai Fung Singapore is not certified halal; consumers should verify with the restaurant directly. 9. is_vegetarian is false because the soup typically contains egg and may use chicken stock. 10. The food_id GD000607 is preserved from the input seed record.
